title: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2025
short_title: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill renews and updates a 1998 law that helps the government study and respond to harmful algal blooms and low oxygen levels in water. These problems can kill fish, hurt the environment, and make people sick when they swim in or drink contaminated water. The bill requires government agencies to work together and create better plans every five years to monitor and predict when these water problems will happen. It also sets up special monitoring systems across the country and provides funding for research and response activities through 2030.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Amendments to the Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Act of 1998
sections.text: This section makes changes to an existing law from nineteen ninety eight about harmful algal blooms and hypoxia. Harmful algal blooms are when too much algae grows in water and can be dangerous. Hypoxia is when water does not have enough oxygen for fish and other sea life to survive. The changes include adding the Department of Energy to a group of agencies that work together on these problems. The changes require this group to create a plan every five years that looks at harmful algal blooms in all types of water including the Great Lakes, rivers, lakes, and coastal waters. The plan must study what causes these blooms, how they affect wildlife and people, and how much money they cost. It must also look at ways to prevent and control these blooms. The group must work with states, Indian tribes, local governments, businesses like fishing and farming, schools, and other organizations when making these plans. The law creates new duties for the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration to watch for and predict harmful blooms and low oxygen events. It also gives the Environmental Protection Agency new jobs to study freshwater harmful blooms. The law sets up a network to monitor these problems across the country and creates a program to test new ways to prevent and control harmful blooms. It provides money for these activities from two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty. Nineteen and a half million dollars per year goes to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and eight million dollars per year goes to the Environmental Protection Agency.

sections.title: Section 3  Other Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Matters
sections.text: This section changes an existing law about declaring harmful algal bloom and hypoxia events as nationally significant. When the federal government decides an event is nationally significant, it can provide extra help and money to affected areas. The changes make it easier for federal officials to waive the requirement that states and local areas pay part of the costs if they cannot reasonably afford to do so. The law allows federal agencies to make contracts and agreements with states, Indian tribes, local governments, and other groups to pay for costs related to these events. It expands who can request that an event be declared nationally significant to include Indian tribe leaders and officials from territories. The law gives more detailed rules about what factors officials should consider when deciding if an event is nationally significant, including risks to public health, environmental damage, economic impacts, effects on people who depend on fishing and hunting for food, and how widespread the event is. The section provides two million dollars per year from two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty to carry out these activities.
